preamble: The transformation of the voiced velar plosive /g/ into the voiced uvular fricative /ʁ/ is a documented phonetic shift involving both a change in manner of articulation (lenition) and a retraction of the place of articulation (uvularization). This process often occurs in specific phonetic environments, such as before back vowels, or as a regular sound change within a language's historical development.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (plosive to fricative), Backing (velar to uvular), Uvularization

examples.note: Regular sound change from /g/ to uvular fricatives in Amdo lects.
Regular sound change from /g/ to uvular fricatives.
Attested backing of /g/ to [ʁ] as in [øɑpoʁotʃi] from <ʔǫą-poĝó-ci>.

sources: The uvularization of g-/d- in Tibetic languages.
Oberly, J. (2008). A Phonetic Analysis of Southern Ute with a Focus on Vowel Quality and Prosody.
Yeou, M., & Maeda, S. (2011). Airflow and acoustic modelling of pharyngeal and uvular consonants in Moroccan Arabic. Instrumental Studies in Arabic Phonetics, 141-158.
